tt_insn_vec.help: Portion of vector registers that is actually used. Available only for instructions belonging to a SIMD/vector instructions set (like x86 AVX)

tt_Recip_throughput.help: Average number of cycles to retire that instruction if repeated (assuming independent instructions, with no read-after-write dependency)

hint.workaround:
- Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
 - Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
hint.details:
- Constant non-unit stride: 8 occurrence(s)
Non-unit stride (uncontiguous) accesses are not efficiently using data caches
hint.title: Slow data structures access
hint.txt: Detected data structures (typically arrays) that cannot be efficiently read/written

hint.title: Type of elements and instruction set
hint.txt: 15 SSE or AVX instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in scalar mode (one at a time).

hint.title: Matching between your loop (in the source code) and the binary loop
hint.txt:
The binary loop is composed of 18 FP arithmetical operations:
 - 14: addition or subtraction (3 inside FMA instructions)
 - 4: multiply (3 inside FMA instructions)
The binary loop is loading 128 bytes (16 double precision FP elements).
The binary loop is storing 8 bytes (1 double precision FP elements).

hint.title: Arithmetic intensity
hint.txt: Arithmetic intensity is 0.13 FP operations per loaded or stored byte.

expert.help: Compare the CQA-computed load/store/compute metrics with the capacity of the core. For instance if CQA computes 24 bytes in the loop in 2 cycles (that is 12 cycles per cycle) and if the core can process up to 16 bytes per cycle, that metric is 75%.
expert.title: Cycles and memory resources usage
expert.txt:
Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each iteration of the binary loop takes 5.67 cycles. At this rate:
 - 23% of peak load performance is reached (22.59 out of 96.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
 - 2% of peak store performance is reached (1.41 out of 64.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))

expert.help: List of bottlenecks detected by CQA. A bottleneck is an issue which reduces front-end throughput below the core capacity.
expert.title: Front-end bottlenecks

header: 13% of peak computational performance is used (3.18 out of 24.00 FLOP per cycle (GFLOPS @ 1GHz))

gain.workaround:
- use profile-guided optimization flags of your compiler
 - hardcode most frequent values of loop bounds by adding specialized paths.:
  *  For instance, replace do i=1,n foo(i) with:
select case (n)
  case (4): do i=1,4 foo(i)
  case (6): do i=1,6 foo(i)
  default : do i=1,n foo(i)
end select
gain.details: The more iterations the main loop is processing, the higher the trip count must be to amortize peel/tail overhead.
gain.title: Unrolling/vectorization cost
gain.txt: This loop is peel/tail of a unrolled/vectorized loop. If its cost is not negligible compared to the main (unrolled/vectorized) loop, unrolling/vectorization is counterproductive due to low trip count.

gain.details: All SSE/AVX instructions are used in scalar version (process only one data element in vector registers).
gain.title: Vectorization
gain.txt:
Your loop is not vectorized.
8 data elements could be processed at once in vector registers.
<<image_1x64_512>>

gain.workaround:
- Read less array elements
 - Write less array elements
 - Provide more information to your compiler:
  * hardcode the bounds of the corresponding 'for' loop
gain.title: Execution units bottlenecks
gain.txt:
Performance is limited by:
 - reading data from caches/RAM (load units are a bottleneck)
 - writing data to caches/RAM (the store unit is a bottleneck)

potential.workaround:
Try to change order in which elements are evaluated (using parentheses) in arithmetic expressions containing both ADD/SUB and MUL operations to enable your compiler to generate FMA instructions wherever possible.
For instance a + b*c is a valid FMA (MUL then ADD).
However (a+b)* c cannot be translated into an FMA (ADD then MUL).
potential.title: FMA
potential.txt:
Detected 3 FMA (fused multiply-add) operations.
Presence of both ADD/SUB and MUL operations.

 - Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
